Dear Mr. Bamberg: 
September 13, 1985 
Re= 
Our File No. A-85-l74 
I apologize for the delay in responding to your letter of 
August 6, 1985, which requested advice with respect to the cam-
paign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act (Gov. 
Code Sections 81000-91015). 
Specifically, you asked whether the 
Mission Hills Democratic Club is receiving "contributions" as 
defined by the Act. 
FPPC regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215 (copy enclosed) 
defines the term "contribution" as "any monetary or nonmonetary 
payment made for political purposes for which full and adequate 
consideration is not made to the donor." 
The regulation also 
states that a payment is made for "political purposes" if it is: 
For the purpose of influencing or attempting to 
influence the action of the voters for or against 
the nomination or election of a candidate or 
candidates, or the qualification or passage of 
any measure. 
As I told you during our telephone conversation today, all of the 
activities described in your letter fall within this definition 
and, therefore, the Mission Hills Democratic Club is a "recipient 
connnittee" pursuant to Section 820l3(a) of the Political Reform 
Act. 
In addition, because the Club has received a charter from 
the San Diego County Central Connnittee, it qualifies as "an 
official connnittee of a political party" pursuant to subsection 
(a)(2)(C) of Regulation 18215. 
Enclosed please find the FPPC Information Manual on Campaign 
Disclosure Provisions of the Political Reform Act along with the 
disclosure forms the Club will need to file. 


Daniel F. Bamberg 
September 13, 1985 
Page 2 
If you need additional information or assistance, please 
do not hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5662 or the Commission's 
Los Angeles office at (213) 620-5196. 
Sincerely, 
Carla Wardlow 
Political Reform Consultant

BYLAWS 
OF 
MISSION HILLS 
DEMOrRATIC CLUB OF SAN DIEGO rOUNTY 
ARTICLE I 
NAME 
The name of the organization shall be the"Mission Hills 
Democratic Club of San Diego County". 
ARTICLE II 
GEOGRAPHICAL DESIGNATION FOR RESIDENT MEMBERBHIP 
Mission Hills, as defined for purposes of the Mission Hills 
Democratic Club, shall be bounded on the North by Interstate 8, 
on the East by Interstate 163, on the South by Laurel Street and 
on the West by Interstate 5. 
ARTICLE III 
PURPOSE 
The purpose of the Mission Hills Democratic Club is to 
foster the ideals of the Democratic Party by stimulating an 
active interest in the Democratic Party; providing a constructive 
role for the volunteer in Democratic Politics; and contributing 
to party leadership and responsibility, promoting harmony within 
the party, supporting the party platform and electing democratic 
candidates in all elections, partisan and non-partisan, local, 
State-wide and national. 
ARTICLE IV 
MEMBERSHIP 
Section 1. 
Types of Membership. 
There shall be three categorles of membership 
in the Mission Hills Democratic rlub: resident, associate 
and sustalning. 
Section 2. 
Resident Membership. 
A person residing in Mission Hills who is a 
voter, registered as a Democrat or, if unable to register to 
vote, has expressed the intent to register to vote as a 
Democrat, shall be eligible for resident membership status 
in the Mission Hliis Democratic Club.

ARTICLE VI 
VOTING 
Section 1. 
Eligibility To Vote. 
Only those resident and non-resl.dent 
memberships, current in their payment of dues may vote. 
Section 2. 
One Vote Per Membership. 
A membership held in the name of one or more 
individuals shall be entitled to one vote. 
Sectl.on 3. 
Proxy And Absentee Voting. 
No proxy voting or absentee ballots shall be 
permitted for endorsement of candidates for poll.tl.cal 
offl.ce. Proxy voting and absentee ballots shall be accepted 
for proposed changes in the club's Bylaws, Resolutions and

Section 4.

Except for amendments to these Bylaws, whl.ch 
shall require a two-thirds vote, all other votes taken of

For purposes of this 
Section, a member castl.ng an absentee ballot or a proxy

Elected Officers.

The Treasurer shall prepare a

Sectl0n 3. 
Elections. 
At the March general meetlng the Presldent

The 
Nominating Committee shall select at least one candidate for

ThlS report shall constitute nominations, and

Those members nominated at the March general 
meeting shall be voted upon at the June general meeting by

Those nominees who recelve a majority vote 
shall be declared elected. 
If no person receives a majority 
of votes cast, a run-off shall be held between those two 
persons receiving the greatest number of votes and the 
person who thereupon recelves a majority of votes cast shall 
be declared elected. 
Sect10n 4.

Should any off1cer fall to attend three

Any vacant elect1ve off1ce shall be filled by

If the club does 
not ratify the choice of the Executive Committee, then

If more than one person is nominated, then election w111 be 
by secret ballot and the person receiving majority of votes 
cast shall be declared elected. 
Section 5. 
Appo1nted Officers. 
The Pres1dent shall appo1nt a parliamentar1an 
and any other officers not otherwise specif1ed by these

ARTICLE VIII 
STANDING COMMITTEES 
The MisSion Hills Democratic Club shall have the 
follOWing standing commi ttees: F1nance, (and) Membersh~p; 
Section 1. 
Finance. 
The F1nance Committee shall be cha1red by the 
Treasurer and comprised of at least five other members 
appo1nted by the Treasurer and ratif1ed by the Execut1ve 
Comm1ttee. 
The Finance Committee shall be responsible for 
all fundraising activi ties and for insur1ng that the Mission 
Hills Democratic Club adheres to sound financial practices. 
Sect ion 2. 
Membersh1p. 
The Membership Committee shall be chaired by 
the Secretary and comprised of at least five other members 
appo1nted by the Secretary and rat1fied by the Executive 
Commi ttee. 
The Membersh1p Comm1 ttee shall be responsible 
for ma1nta1n1ng a current list of the membership, obta1n1ng 
new members, 
and publishing and distributing a 
M1ssion H11ls Democratic Club Newsletter to the membersh1p 
at least four t1mes per year. 
Sect10n 3. 
Events. 
The Events Committee shall be cha1red by the 
Adm1nistrat1ve Vice-Pres1dent and compr1sed of at least f1ve 
other members appo1nted by the Adm1n1strat1ve V1ce-Pres1dent 
and rat1fied by the Executive Comm1ttee. 
The Events 
Committee shall be responsible for schedul1ng at least four

the general membersh1p and such other

to t1me schedule.

., 
Section 4. 
Execut1ve. 
The Executive Committee shall be cha1red by 
the President and comprised of all elected officers. 
The 
Executive Committee shall be responsible for carrying out 
the club's business between general meetings 1n a manner not 
1nconsistent with actions taken at general meetings and any 
other duties specified in these Bylaws or in Roberts' Rules 
of Order, Newly Revised. 
The President shall call a meeting of the 
Execut1ve Committee at least once a month, however a meeting 
may be called by a major1ty of elected officers. 
The quorum 
at an Execut1ve Committee meeting is a majority of elected 
off1cers. 
If immediate action is needed, the Executive 
Committee may act by telephonic vote, but such actions are 
subject to ratificat10n at an Executive committee meet1ng to 
be called as soon thereafter as feasible. 
ARTICLE VIX 
MEETINGS 
Sect10n 1. 
General Meet1ngs. 
There shall be at least four general meetings 
of the membership per year. 
General meet1ngs shall be held 
at a t1me and place spec1f1ed by the Execut1ve Committee. 
Section 2. 
Quorum. 
A quorum at a general meeting shall be ten 
percent (10%) of the club's resident and assoc1ate members 
in good standing. 
Sect10n 3. 
Parliamentary Authority. 
Robert's Rules of Order, Newly Revised, shall 
be the parliamentary author1ty for the club on all questions 
not covered by the Bylaws. 
ARTICLE X 
NOTICE 
At least seven days pr10r to the general meet1ng, the 
Secretary shall cause to be deposited into the regular Un1ted 
States ma11 a newsletter or not~ce addressed to each member in

The newsletter or not1ce shall 
spec1fy the time, place and date of the general meet1ng, proposed

ARTICLE XI 
CANDIDATE ENDORSEMENT 
The club may endorse and/or contribute financially to 
Democratic candidates in partisan elections or any candidate in a 
a non-partisan election, as directed by a majority vote at a 
general meeting provided that notice is given as specified in 
Article IX, of these Bylaws. 
ARTICLE XII 
AFFILIATION 
The club shall affiliate as an associate member of the 
San Diego County Democratic Central Committee and pay the current 
annual fee. 
ARTICLE XIII 
AMENDMENT 
These Bylaws may be amended by a two-thirds vote at a 
general meeting after such amendment has been presented at the 
general meeting the preceding vote and notice is provided as 
specified in Article IX of these Bylaws. 
ARTICLE XIV 
ADOPTION 
These Bylaws shall become effective immediately upon their 
adoption and shall supercede any other Constitution, Bylaws, and 
any other document of this club with which it is inconsistent. 
Adopted on March 14th, 1985 at the 
first regular meeting of the 
Mission Hills Democratic Club.

sections.question: Specifically, you asked whether the Mission Hills Democratic Club is receiving "contributions" as defined by the Act.
sections.conclusion: As I told you during our telephone conversation today, all of the activities described in your letter fall within this definition and, therefore, the Mission Hills Democratic Club is a "recipient committee" pursuant to Section 82013(a) of the Political Reform Act. In addition, because the Club has received a charter from the San Diego County Central Committee, it qualifies as "an official committee of a political party" pursuant to subsection (a)(2)(C) of Regulation 18215.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the Mission Hills Democratic Club, a recently formed Democratic Party organization that collects membership dues, publishes a newsletter to registered Democrats, holds nomination conventions, endorses candidates, and intends to contribute to endorsed candidates' campaigns, is receiving "contributions" as defined by the Political Reform Act and what are its reporting obligations.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Mission Hills Democratic Club is a recipient committee under the Political Reform Act because its described activities fall within the definition of making payments for political purposes. Additionally, as a chartered official committee of a political party, it must comply with applicable disclosure and filing requirements.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter (dated August 6, 1985) and the FPPC response letter (dated September 13, 1985). The incoming request includes the club's bylaws as enclosure. FPPC response clearly identifies the question asked and provides definitive conclusion. Minor OCR errors present in original document (e.g., 'connnittee' with triple-n) but content is clear.

QUESTION: Specifically, you asked whether the Mission Hills Democratic Club is receiving "contributions" as defined by the Act.

CONCLUSION: As I told you during our telephone conversation today, all of the activities described in your letter fall within this definition and, therefore, the Mission Hills Democratic Club is a "recipient committee" pursuant to Section 82013(a) of the Political Reform Act. In addition, because the Club has received a charter from the San Diego County Central Committee, it qualifies as "an official committee of a political party" pursuant to subsection (a)(2)(C) of Regulation 18215.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to the Mission Hills Democratic Club's inquiry confirming that the club's membership dues, newsletter distribution to registered Democrats, candidate endorsements, and intended contributions to endorsed candidates constitute activities receiving "contributions" under the Political Reform Act. The club is classified as a recipient committee and official party committee subject to campaign disclosure requirements and must file appropriate forms.
